text: Post-exercise massage has small overall effects on performance recovery, with effects being larger for short-term recovery within 10 minutes (+7.9%, g = 0.45) compared to recovery periods over 20 minutes (+2.4%, g = 0.08).
specific_value: +7.9% (g = 0.45) for ≤10 min recovery vs +2.4% (g = 0.08) for >20 min

text: Shorter massage sessions of 5-12 minutes produce larger performance recovery effects (+6.6%, g = 0.34) than sessions lasting more than 12 minutes (+1.0%, g = 0.06).
Massage is most effective for recovery after high-intensity mixed exercise (+14.4%, g = 0.61), with smaller effects after strength exercise (+3.9%, g = 0.18) and endurance exercise (+1.3%, g = 0.12).
Untrained individuals tend to benefit more from post-exercise massage (+6.5%, g = 0.23) than trained athletes (+2.3%, g = 0.17).
The performance recovery benefit of massage after endurance exercise is minimal (+1.3%, g = 0.12), questioning its widespread use as a recovery tool for competitive endurance athletes.
For short-term recovery after intensive mixed training sessions, massage can provide meaningful performance benefits and may be a justified recovery intervention.
